global.practical_note: Order-of-magnitude range reported across the literature spans ~10^5 to ~10^8 Gt CO2 (Kelemen et al. 2011, 2019) depending on whether the full peridotite/basalt rock volume within a few km of the surface is assumed reactive. NAS (2019) and Snæbjörnsdóttir et al. (2020) treat ~10^6 Gt as an order-of-magnitude 'rock volume x stoichiometry' theoretical ceiling — several orders of magnitude larger than any credible injectivity- or economics-limited practical capacity. No aggregated bottom-up global total (summing individually assessed formations) yet exists in the peer-reviewed literature; national/regional assessments (this file) cover only a small, non-representative fraction of global mafic/ultramafic rock volume.
sources: Snæbjörnsdóttir, S.Ó. et al. 2020. Carbon dioxide storage through mineral carbonation. Nature Reviews Earth & Environment 1, 90-102. https://www.nature.com/articles/s43017-019-0011-8
Kelemen, P.B. & Matter, J. 2008. In situ carbonation of peridotite for CO2 storage. PNAS 105(45):17295-17300. https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.0805794105
Kelemen, P.B. et al. 2011/2019 syntheses cited in NASEM 2019 give 10^5-10^8 Gt CO2 peridotite theoretical range.

National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine (NASEM) 2019. Negative Emissions Technologies and Reliable Sequestration: A Research Agenda, Ch. 6 Carbon Mineralization of CO2. https://nap.nationalacademies.org/read/25259/chapter/8

formations.name: Columbia River Basalt Group (Grande Ronde / Wallula)
formations.capacity_basis: McGrail et al. 2006 conservative regional estimate 10-50 Gt CO2 (an earlier, less-cited McGrail 2006 figure of ~100 Gt also circulates). Later PNNL (2024, ScienceDirect 'Gigaton commercial-scale carbon storage...stacked Columbia River basalt reservoirs') estimates 36-148 Gt CO2 for the onshore Columbia River Basalt specifically (with much larger 500-2500 Gt offshore WA/BC and 1000-5500 Gt Caribbean flood-basalt figures reported in the same study as separate, more speculative provinces). High bound revised from 100 to 148 Gt to match the 2024 PNNL range; verified via web search July 2026.
formations.depth_porosity_notes: Multiple stacked flood-basalt flows (Grande Ronde, Wanapum, Saddle Mountains) with permeable brecciated flow-top/interflow zones at 800-900+ m depth at Wallula; porosity concentrated in interflow rubble zones, dense flow interiors act as seals/reactive caprock.
formations.suitability_notes: Best-characterized onshore basalt storage target globally; demonstrated at pilot scale.
formations.existing_activity: Wallula Basalt Pilot (PNNL/Big Sky Carbon Sequestration Partnership): 977 t CO2 injected 2013; post-injection coring found up to 60% mineralized as ankerite within ~2 years. Follow-on 'Carbon TrapRock'/CarbonSAFE studies ongoing.
formations.source: McGrail, B.P. et al. 2006 (AGU); McGrail et al. 2017 Env. Sci. Technol. Wallula results; ScienceDirect 2024 'Gigaton commercial-scale carbon storage and mineralization potential in stacked Columbia River basalt reservoirs' https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S175058362400149X

formations.name: Deccan Traps
formations.capacity_basis: Vishal et al. (2021) estimate ~316 Gt CO2 combined Deccan Volcanic Province + Rajmahal Traps; subsequent reassessments (2022-2025, e.g. Princeton CMI summary, Discover Geoscience 2025) narrow practically accessible capacity to ~94-150 Gt given shallow depth and dense dyke networks that limit reservoir quality. A further probabilistic reassessment (2021-2025 systematic capacity assessment work incorporating porosity/net-to-gross/volumetric uncertainty) narrows this further to ~41-76 Gt CO2 (P10=43, P50=58, P90=74 Gt), suggesting the practical range may be lower than the 94-150 Gt figure retained here; flagged in verification_notes.
formations.depth_porosity_notes: Traps are up to ~2 km thick in the Western Ghats but reactive, porous flow-top zones are concentrated in the uppermost few hundred meters; many areas too shallow (<800 m) for supercritical CO2 injection; extensive vertical dyke swarms compartmentalize flow.
formations.suitability_notes: Large theoretical resource but 'virtually none of it appears suitable for large-scale CO2 injection' per Princeton CMI (2022) synthesis of Indian basalt studies, due to shallow depth/dyke compartmentalization; targeted formations (e.g. Eastern Deccan Volcanic Province, Killari-1 borehole area) show more promise.
formations.existing_activity: Laboratory carbonation experiments (CSIR-NGRI, IIT Bombay) on Deccan basalt cores; no field-scale pilot injection yet as of 2026.
formations.source: Vishal, V. et al. 2021 capacity assessment; Princeton CMI 2022 'India's Deccan Traps Appear to Have Limited Capacity for Carbon Storage' https://cmi.princeton.edu/annual-meetings/annual-reports/year-2022-1/indias-deccan-traps-appear-to-have-limited-capacity-for-carbon-storage/; Discover Geoscience 2025 https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44288-025-00259-7

formations.name: Rajmahal Traps
formations.capacity_basis: Included within combined ~316 Gt Vishal et al. 2021 Deccan+Rajmahal estimate; no disaggregated figure published.
formations.depth_porosity_notes: Smaller, eastern Indian flood basalt province (~/100 m thick lava pile), Early Cretaceous age, less studied than Deccan.
formations.suitability_notes: Minor target relative to Deccan; regional CO2 mineral trapping experiments (Eastern Deccan Volcanic Province studies) sample nearby basalts.
formations.existing_activity: No known pilot.
formations.source: Vishal, V. et al. 2021; ScienceDirect 2013 'CO2 Mineral Trapping: An Experimental Study on the Carbonation of Basalts from the Eastern Deccan Volcanic Province, India' https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1878522013001501

formations.name: Siberian Traps
formations.capacity_basis: No dedicated peer-reviewed CO2-storage capacity assessment identified; would be enormous given areal extent (largest continental flood basalt province on Earth, ~2-4 million km2 original extent) but is essentially unstudied for CCS/mineralization purposes.
formations.depth_porosity_notes: Up to 3400 m thick lava pile in places (Tunguska Basin); permafrost terrain and remoteness are major practical obstacles.
formations.suitability_notes: Largest LIP globally by volume; a major data gap for the global inventory. No known industrial CO2 source co-location or infrastructure.
formations.existing_activity: None known.
formations.source: General LIP geology literature (Reichow et al. 2009 Earth Planet. Sci. Lett. on Siberian Traps volume/age); no mineralization-specific capacity study found.

formations.name: Paraná-Etendeka Large Igneous Province
formations.capacity_basis: No basin-wide Gt figure yet published; Rossetti et al. 2025 (Basin Research) and 2024 EAGE paper provide subsurface geophysical characterization framing capacity work, and laboratory work (Scientific Reports 2024) shows ~75% carbonation efficiency (aragonite/dolomite/calcite) within 72 hours in flow-through experiments, supporting favorable reaction kinetics.
formations.depth_porosity_notes: South American (Paraná) portion ~1.2 x10^6 km2, ~1.5 km average thickness (up to 2 km), Early Cretaceous tholeiitic basalt; interflow/vesicular zones provide reservoir-quality intervals; African conjugate (Etendeka, Namibia/Angola) smaller, more eroded.
formations.suitability_notes: Emerging CCS target given proximity to Brazilian/Argentine industrial CO2 sources (steel, cement, refineries) and rapid experimental carbonation kinetics; subsurface stratigraphic mapping underway (2024-2025) as precursor to capacity estimates.
formations.existing_activity: No field pilot yet; active subsurface geophysical/stratigraphic characterization program (2024-2025).
formations.source: Rossetti, L. et al. 2025 Basin Research 'Subsurface Geology of the Paraná-Etendeka LIP' https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/bre.70038; Scientific Reports 2024 'Unraveling the rapid CO2 mineralization experiment using the Paraná flood basalts' https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-58729-w

formations.name: Karoo Large Igneous Province
formations.capacity_basis: No published mineralization capacity estimate identified. Original Karoo/Ferrar LIP extent (with Antarctica) estimated at >3 x10^6 km2 but present-day exposed/preserved Karoo basalt (Drakensberg Group, Lesotho highlands) is much smaller (~200,000 km2).
formations.depth_porosity_notes: Up to 1.4 km thick lava pile in Lesotho highlands (Drakensberg basalts), Early Jurassic age; limited subsurface reservoir characterization for CCS purposes.
formations.suitability_notes: Major data gap; South Africa has significant coal-fired CO2 point sources (Mpumalanga) that could pair with Karoo basalt/dolerite sills, but no dedicated CCS-in-basalt studies found.
formations.existing_activity: None known; South African CCS research (SACCCS) has focused primarily on sedimentary basins, not Karoo basalt mineralization.
formations.source: General Karoo LIP volcanology literature (Duncan & Marsh 2006); no mineralization capacity study identified.

formations.name: Ethiopian-Yemen Traps / Afar
formations.capacity_basis: No dedicated capacity study found; Oligocene Ethiopian/Yemen flood basalts (~30 Ma) plus ongoing Afar rift volcanism give both LIP-scale flood basalt (flood_basalt) and active rift basalt (rift_volcanic) targets - see separate 'East African Rift - Ethiopia/Djibouti' entry for the rift-related component.
formations.depth_porosity_notes: Up to 2 km thick trap sequence in NW Ethiopian highlands; highly fractured Afar rift basalts at shallower depth near the rift axis.
formations.suitability_notes: Large areal extent, essentially unstudied for CCS; overlaps with active East African Rift basalt category below.
formations.existing_activity: None known for mineralization specifically.
formations.source: General geology (Hofmann et al. 1997 on Ethiopian flood basalt volcanism); no CO2 storage capacity study identified.

formations.name: North Atlantic Igneous Province (Faroe Islands / Vøring & Møre margins)
formations.capacity_basis: No basin-wide Gt figure yet published for the NAIP specifically; the broader claim that 'mid-ocean-ridge and oceanic basalt systems worldwide could store up to ~100,000 Gt CO2' (SINTEF/NORDICCS synthesis) is sometimes applied to NAIP margins by extension, but this is a global-ridge figure, not an NAIP-specific assessment.
formations.depth_porosity_notes: Faroe Islands Basalt Group reservoir study (Rosenqvist et al. 2023) finds porosity 0.5-36.2% concentrated in brecciated/vesicular flow-top crusts (individual flows 0.6-18.5 m thick at the 642E borehole, Vøring Margin); permeability up to ~10^-12 m2 in least-altered crusts; more altered basalt is markedly less reactive/permeable.
formations.suitability_notes: Targeted by Norwegian (Njord/CO2Basalt project, University of Oslo) research as a novel offshore CCS reservoir type distinct from saline aquifers; would pair with North Sea/Norwegian CO2 capture hubs (Northern Lights etc.) if developed.
formations.existing_activity: Research-stage characterization only (CO2Basalt project, Njord Centre, Univ. of Oslo); no injection pilot.
formations.source: Rosenqvist, M.P. et al. 2023, 'Reservoir Properties and Reactivity of the Faroe Islands Basalt Group', Int. J. Greenhouse Gas Control, https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1750583623000087; Njord CO2Basalt project https://www.mn.uio.no/njord/english/research/projects/co2-basalts/index.html; Geoforskning.no 'Can we store CO2 in Norway's offshore lava flows?'

formations.name: Emeishan Large Igneous Province
formations.capacity_basis: No dedicated CO2 mineralization capacity study identified in literature searched.
formations.depth_porosity_notes: Permian flood basalt province in SW China (Sichuan/Yunnan/Guizhou), up to 5 km thick in the inner zone; poorly characterized for CCS reservoir properties.
formations.suitability_notes: Data gap; China has strong CCS/CCUS policy interest but published work concentrates on sedimentary basin storage (Ordos, Sichuan Basin sandstones), not Emeishan basalt mineralization.
formations.existing_activity: None known.
formations.source: General LIP literature (Xu et al. 2004 on Emeishan volcanism); no capacity study identified.

formations.name: Central Atlantic Magmatic Province (CAMP) - Eastern North America / Morocco
formations.capacity_basis: No dedicated capacity estimate identified; CAMP (Triassic-Jurassic, ~201 Ma) is one of the largest LIPs by original areal extent (>10^7 km2 including now-separated continents) but present-day exposed basalt (Newark, Hartford, Culpeper basin traps in eastern USA; Morocco's High Atlas basalts) is comparatively limited and thin (100s of m).
formations.depth_porosity_notes: Eastern US CAMP basalts (e.g. Watchung/Palisades) are typically <300 m thick, often near-surface (unsuitable depth for supercritical CO2 without deeper equivalents); Moroccan CAMP basalts similarly thin.
formations.suitability_notes: Likely limited practical relevance vs. other LIPs due to thinness/shallow depth, but included for completeness given proximity to dense US East Coast CO2 sources.
formations.existing_activity: None known.
formations.source: General CAMP literature (Marzoli et al. 1999 Science); no mineralization capacity study identified.

formations.name: Samail Ophiolite (Oman / UAE)
formations.capacity_basis: Kelemen & Matter 2008 PNAS: ~50x10^12 tonnes of peridotite in the Samail massif could mineralize ~30x10^12 t CO2 (30,000 Gt) if fully carbonated; wider literature range (Kelemen et al. 2011/2019 syntheses) of 10^4-10^5 Gt depending on assumed reactive fraction/depth cutoff.
formations.depth_porosity_notes: World's largest and best-exposed on-land ophiolite (obducted oceanic crust+mantle); peridotite (harzburgite/dunite) exposed at surface to several km depth; natural carbonation already forms travertine terraces and carbonate veins (14C ages ~26,000 yr average, up to Holocene) demonstrating rapid natural reaction kinetics; also present offshore beneath thin sediment cover in the Gulf of Oman.
formations.suitability_notes: The flagship global ISM target in the literature; natural CO2 uptake estimated to already consume >10,000-100,000 t CO2/yr passively, with in-situ enhanced carbonation (fracturing, heating, elevated pCO2) proposed to reach >1 Gt CO2/yr in Oman alone (Kelemen & Matter 2008).
formations.existing_activity: 44.01 (Omani startup, backed by Actis, XPRIZE Carbon Removal winner 2023) actively injecting CO2 into peridotite at pilot scale in the Fizh/Samail massif; academic Oman Drilling Project boreholes used for carbonation-rate studies.
formations.source: Kelemen, P.B. & Matter, J. 2008. PNAS 105(45):17295-300 https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.0805794105; Kelemen et al. 2011 Annu. Rev. Earth Planet. Sci.; Mervine et al. 2014 GCA (14C carbonation ages) https://www.ldeo.columbia.edu/sites/default/files/u988/2014%20Mervine%20et%20al%20GCA%2014C%20Oman%20carbonates.pdf; JPT/SPE 2023 coverage of 44.01

formations.name: New Caledonia Peridotite Nappe
formations.capacity_basis: Kelemen et al. (Frontiers 2019 and related syntheses) note the New Caledonia massif (~150 x 40 km) is one of the world's largest peridotite bodies; converting all Mg cations to carbonate across combined large ophiolites (New Caledonia + PNG) discussed at the ~77,000 Gt (77x10^12 t) scale in aggregate, but no New-Caledonia-specific disaggregated number published.
formations.depth_porosity_notes: Obducted oceanic mantle peridotite (harzburgite/dunite), extensively serpentinized and lateritized at surface (source of world-class Ni ore); documented active natural carbonation (magnesite veins, offshore alkaline carbonate-chimney spring) since the Oligocene.
formations.suitability_notes: Strong natural analogue for in-situ carbonation given documented active magnesite-forming systems; offshore extension beneath thin sediment noted by Kelemen et al. as advantageous for injection infrastructure siting.
formations.existing_activity: No CCS pilot; studied academically for natural carbonation processes (meteoric-water-driven listwanite/magnesite formation).
formations.source: Kelemen, P.B. et al. Frontiers 2019 status/challenges review https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/climate/articles/10.3389/fclim.2019.00009/full; Quesnel, B. et al. 2013 'Syn-tectonic, meteoric water-derived carbonation of the New Caledonia Peridotite Nappe'

formations.name: Papua New Guinea Ophiolite Belt
formations.capacity_basis: Cited by Kelemen et al. as one of the world's largest ophiolites (~200 x 50 km, Papuan Ultramafic Belt); no disaggregated Gt figure published separately from Oman/New Caledonia comparisons.
formations.depth_porosity_notes: Papuan Ultramafic Belt - obducted Cretaceous oceanic crust/mantle sequence; peridotite present beneath thin sedimentary cover offshore, similar to Oman and New Caledonia.
formations.suitability_notes: Remote, limited infrastructure; flagged in the academic literature as geologically world-class but essentially undeveloped for CCS.
formations.existing_activity: None known.
formations.source: Kelemen, P.B. et al. Frontiers 2019 https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/climate/articles/10.3389/fclim.2019.00009/full

formations.name: Zambales Ophiolite (Philippines)
formations.capacity_basis: No dedicated capacity study found.
formations.depth_porosity_notes: Eocene-Oligocene ophiolite complex (Zambales Range, Luzon) with harzburgite, dunite, and associated chromite deposits; partially serpentinized.
formations.suitability_notes: One of several SE Asian ophiolite belts (with Palawan, Mindanao) that are geologically analogous to Oman but entirely unstudied for CO2 storage as of this compilation.
formations.existing_activity: None known.
formations.source: General Philippine ophiolite geology literature (Yumul et al. reviews); no mineralization study identified.

formations.name: Troodos Ophiolite (Cyprus)
formations.capacity_basis: No basin-scale capacity estimate published; only bench-scale carbonation-efficiency experiments exist (harzburgite ~70% olivine/25% pyroxene/2-3% serpentine; dunite ~97% olivine) showing low carbonation extent for unmilled samples (0.7%) rising sharply with milling (7.4%) in short autoclave tests, illustrating the general kinetic-limitation problem rather than an in-situ reservoir capacity.
formations.depth_porosity_notes: Classic, well-preserved Cretaceous ophiolite; harzburgite and dunite exposed extensively in the Troodos massif; degree of serpentinization varies, controlling in-situ reactivity.
formations.suitability_notes: Long-studied academically as an ophiolite type-locality; no field-scale CO2 injection proposed to date.
formations.existing_activity: Laboratory carbonation-kinetics studies only.
formations.source: General Troodos petrology + peridotite carbonation kinetics literature (autoclave carbonation-efficiency studies of Troodos harzburgite/dunite).

formations.name: Turkish Ophiolites (Kizildag / Anatolian belt)
formations.capacity_basis: No dedicated CO2 storage capacity study identified.
formations.depth_porosity_notes: Multiple Neotethyan ophiolite belts across southern/central Anatolia (Kizildag, Pozanti-Karsanti, others) with harzburgite/dunite bodies, variably serpentinized; some hosting natural travertine/magnesite occurrences indicative of natural carbonation.
formations.suitability_notes: Data gap; geologically comparable to other Neotethyan ophiolites (Oman, Albania) but unstudied for engineered mineralization.
formations.existing_activity: None known.
formations.source: General Turkish ophiolite geology literature; no mineralization capacity study identified.

formations.name: Mirdita Ophiolite (Albania) / Balkan Ophiolite Belt
formations.capacity_basis: No dedicated CO2 storage capacity study identified.
formations.depth_porosity_notes: Jurassic Mirdita ophiolite (Albania) and related Balkan ophiolite fragments (Vourinos-Greece, Pindos) include large harzburgite/dunite massifs, historically mined for chromite; natural listwanite (carbonated serpentinite) occurrences documented.
formations.suitability_notes: Data gap; a plausible future European ISM target given proximity to Balkan industrial CO2 sources, but no capacity work published.
formations.existing_activity: None known.
formations.source: General Balkan ophiolite geology literature; no mineralization capacity study identified.

formations.name: California Coast Range Ophiolite / Serpentinite belt
formations.capacity_basis: No dedicated in-situ CO2 storage capacity study identified; California serpentinite is more often discussed for ex-situ/surficial mineral carbonation (mine tailings, enhanced weathering) than deep in-situ injection.
formations.depth_porosity_notes: Coast Range Ophiolite and associated Franciscan Complex serpentinite bodies (including the historic New Idria and Clear Creek serpentinite masses) are extensively exposed but generally structurally complex/faulted (San Andreas system), complicating deep injection.
formations.suitability_notes: California's serpentinite is the state rock and a widely cited surficial carbonation research target (Project Vesta-style enhanced weathering, mine tailings carbonation), but in-situ deep injection is less studied than in Oman-analogue settings due to active faulting/seismicity concerns.
formations.existing_activity: Surficial/ex-situ carbonation and enhanced-weathering research (e.g. on serpentinite mine tailings); no deep in-situ ISM pilot identified.
formations.source: General California Coast Range Ophiolite geology literature; no in-situ mineralization capacity study identified.

formations.name: Bay of Islands Ophiolite (Newfoundland, Canada)
formations.capacity_basis: No dedicated CO2 storage capacity study identified.
formations.depth_porosity_notes: Classic Ordovician ophiolite complex (harzburgite tectonite plus cumulate sequence), well exposed in Gros Morne National Park; variably serpentinized.
formations.suitability_notes: World-renowned type-locality ophiolite used to define oceanic-crust stratigraphy; no engineered CO2 mineralization work identified, and much of it lies within a protected national park, limiting industrial development potential.
formations.existing_activity: None known.
formations.source: General Bay of Islands ophiolite geology literature; no mineralization capacity study identified.

formations.name: British Columbia Serpentinite Belts (Decar / Cassiar / Shulaps / Atlin)
formations.capacity_basis: No basin-wide Gt figure published; site-selection studies (2024 ScienceDirect 'Evaluation and site selection for carbon storage via shallow CO2 injection into serpentinite in British Columbia') characterize multiple candidate sites (Decar, Cassiar, Shulaps, Coquihalla, Tulameen, Atlin/Bobtail) rather than producing an aggregate capacity number.
formations.depth_porosity_notes: Decar ultramafic body contains up to ~13 wt% brucite (Mg(OH)2) formed during serpentinization - brucite reacts with CO2 even at room temperature, making it a preferred fast-reacting target; fractured serpentinite characterized at multiple sites for near-surface (shallow) CO2 injection rather than deep supercritical storage.
formations.suitability_notes: CO2Lock research program (Geoscience BC-funded) is actively piloting shallow, brucite-targeted mineral carbonation as a lower-cost alternative to deep supercritical CO2 injection; listwanite (carbonated serpentinite/quartz-carbonate rock) at Atlin documents natural analogue carbonation.
formations.existing_activity: CO2Lock pilot-scale shallow CO2 injection trial at the Atlin/Bobtail site; ongoing site characterization (2019-2025) across multiple BC ultramafic bodies.
formations.source: ScienceDirect 2024 'Evaluation and site selection for carbon storage via shallow CO2 injection into serpentinite in British Columbia' https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1750583624001658; Journal of Petrology 2022 'Formation and Preservation of Brucite and Awaruite...Central British Columbia' https://academic.oup.com/petrology/article/63/11/egac100/6747305; ScienceDirect 2025 fractured serpentinite characterization paper https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0883292725002045

formations.name: Thetford Mines / Quebec Asbestos Belt (Appalachian ophiolites)
formations.capacity_basis: No in-situ deep injection capacity study identified; Quebec chrysotile (asbestos) mine tailings have instead been studied extensively as an ex-situ/surficial mineral carbonation feedstock (crushed serpentinite tailings react with atmospheric/flue-gas CO2).
formations.depth_porosity_notes: Cambrian-Ordovician ophiolitic serpentinite (Thetford Mines, Asbestos/Val-des-Sources) associated with legacy chrysotile mining; large volumes of finely milled tailings already exist at surface.
formations.suitability_notes: Primarily an ex-situ mineral carbonation (mine tailings) opportunity rather than a deep in-situ injection target given the milled, surface-stockpiled nature of the resource; included for completeness as part of global ultramafic inventory, capacity distinct in character from deep ISM.
formations.existing_activity: Ex-situ tailings carbonation research (various Canadian universities); no deep in-situ ISM pilot.
formations.source: General Quebec asbestos-belt serpentinite mineral-carbonation literature (tailings carbonation studies).

formations.name: Japan Ophiolites (Hokkaido / Sanbagawa belt)
formations.capacity_basis: No dedicated CO2 mineralization capacity study identified.
formations.depth_porosity_notes: Multiple accreted ophiolite/serpentinite belts across Japan (Hokkaido, Sanbagawa metamorphic belt, Shikoku); tectonically complex, variably serpentinized.
formations.suitability_notes: Data gap; Japan's CCS efforts have focused on offshore sedimentary basins (Tomakomai) rather than onshore ultramafic mineralization.
formations.existing_activity: None known.
formations.source: General Japanese ophiolite geology literature; no mineralization study identified.

formations.name: Kalimantan / Sulawesi Ophiolites (Indonesia)
formations.capacity_basis: No dedicated CO2 mineralization capacity study identified, despite large lateritized peridotite bodies (also major Ni-laterite ore hosts).
formations.depth_porosity_notes: Extensive ultramafic complexes across Central Sulawesi and East Kalimantan (part of the SE Asian ophiolite belt), heavily lateritized at surface; the Mt. Tawai peridotite (Sabah, Malaysia, part of the same regional belt) has been used in lab carbonation-kinetics experiments.
formations.suitability_notes: Large, essentially unassessed ultramafic resource; regional Ni-mining infrastructure could potentially be leveraged for co-located mineralization research.
formations.existing_activity: Bench-scale carbonation experiments on regional peridotite (e.g. Mt. Tawai, Sabah) but no Indonesia-specific in-situ pilot.
formations.source: PMC 2018 'Experimental Investigation and Simplistic Geochemical Modeling of CO2 Mineral Carbonation Using the Mount Tawai Peridotite' https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6273465/

formations.name: Iceland (Carbfix / whole-island basalt)
formations.capacity_basis: Snæbjörnsdóttir & Gislason 2016 (and related NORDICCS/SINTEF synthesis) give theoretical storage potential of onshore + offshore Icelandic basalt of roughly 60-7000 Gt CO2, with the upper end reflecting the full basaltic crust volume rather than practically accessible reservoirs.
formations.depth_porosity_notes: Young (post-glacial, <0.8 Ma), highly porous olivine tholeiite basalt within the active rift zone (~1/3 of Iceland's area) is the most feasible onshore target; older, more altered basalt outside the rift zone is less reactive/permeable.
formations.suitability_notes: The world's leading demonstrated ISM site; Carbfix process dissolves CO2 (and H2S) in water before injection, achieving >95% mineralization within ~2 years at the Hellisheiði pilot/commercial site.
formations.existing_activity: Carbfix (Hellisheiði geothermal plant, operating since 2012) and Coda Terminal (Straumsvík, import-and-store CO2 hub under development) are the most mature ISM operations globally.
formations.source: Snæbjörnsdóttir, S.Ó. & Gislason, S.R. 2016. CO2 storage potential of basaltic rocks in Iceland and the oceanic ridges. Energy Procedia / NORDICCS report https://www.sintef.no/globalassets/sintef-energi/nordiccs/d-6.1.1407-8-co2-storage-potential-of-basaltic-rocks-in-iceland-and-the-oceanic-ridges_web.pdf; Matter, J.M. et al. 2016 Science (CarbFix mineralization rate); Eos.org 'Basalts Turn Carbon into Stone for Permanent Storage'

formations.name: East African Rift - Kenya Rift Valley
formations.capacity_basis: Widely reported (MIT Technology Review 2025, Cella/decarbonfuse coverage) estimated overall Kenyan Rift Valley storage potential of ~400 Gt CO2; Onyango et al. (ScienceDirect 2021, 'Can East African rift basalts sequester CO2? Case study of the Kenya rift') provides the underlying geological case-study framework.
formations.depth_porosity_notes: Quaternary-Neogene rift-related basalts and volcaniclastics near Lake Elementaita/Naivasha, associated with the same geothermal reservoirs (Olkaria, Menengai) that supply Kenya's ~50% geothermal grid; high permeability fracture zones in rift-flank basalt.
formations.suitability_notes: Paired with abundant local geothermal power (low-carbon energy for DAC) - a key economic advantage; water-alternating-gas (WAG, borrowed from EOR) injection technique demonstrated.
formations.existing_activity: Octavia Carbon (DAC) + Cella Mineral Storage: first CO2 mineralization injection in Africa (Project Hummingbird/Jacaranda, near Lake Elementaita), ~460 kg CO2 injected in initial test (2024-2025); 'Great Carbon Valley' scale-up plans reported by MIT Technology Review (Dec 2025).
formations.source: MIT Technology Review Dec 2025 'Welcome to Kenya's Great Carbon Valley' https://www.technologyreview.com/2025/12/22/1130153/geothermal-energy-carbon-capture-kenya-climate-solution/; ScienceDirect 2021 'Can East African rift basalts sequester CO2? Case study of the Kenya rift' https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468227621002283; decarbonfuse.com coverage of Octavia Carbon/Cella injection

formations.name: East African Rift - Ethiopia / Djibouti (Afar Depression)
formations.capacity_basis: No dedicated capacity study identified; the Afar Depression sits atop both the Oligocene Ethiopian/Yemen flood-basalt pile and actively rifting, highly fractured young basalt at the Africa-Arabia-Somali triple junction.
formations.depth_porosity_notes: Very high geothermal gradient and active volcanism (Erta Ale, Dabbahu) provide highly reactive, permeable young basalt, but also challenging drilling conditions (extreme heat, remoteness, seismic/volcanic hazard).
formations.suitability_notes: Geologically analogous to the Kenya rift and Iceland (active rift basalt) but essentially unstudied for CCS; flagged as a plausible future extension of the Kenya rift ISM model.
formations.existing_activity: None known for CO2 mineralization; geothermal exploration ongoing (Djibouti, Ethiopia).
formations.source: General Afar Depression volcanology/rift literature; no CO2 mineralization capacity study identified.

formations.name: Hawaii (Island of Hawaii basalt shield volcanoes)
formations.capacity_basis: No dedicated in-situ mineralization capacity study identified; discussion of Hawaiian basalt in the CCS literature is largely conceptual (young, highly porous ocean-island tholeiite/alkalic basalt analogous to Iceland).
formations.depth_porosity_notes: Extremely young, highly permeable shield-volcano basalt (Mauna Loa, Kilauea) with abundant vesicular flow tops and lava tubes; active volcanic/seismic hazard is a major siting constraint.
formations.suitability_notes: Geologically promising analogue to Iceland's young rift basalt, but active-volcano hazard and lack of large local point-source CO2 emitters limit near-term relevance; a research/data gap.
formations.existing_activity: None known.
formations.source: General Hawaiian volcanology; no mineralization capacity study identified.

formations.name: Japan / Kurile Volcanic Arc Basalts
formations.capacity_basis: No dedicated capacity study identified; arc basalts/andesites are generally less Mg-rich and thus less reactive per unit volume than rift tholeiites or peridotite.
formations.depth_porosity_notes: Calc-alkaline to tholeiitic arc volcanics, variable porosity; Japan's main CCS focus (Tomakomai) targets offshore sedimentary aquifers, not arc basalt mineralization.
formations.suitability_notes: Lower priority ISM target given less favorable (more silicic, less Mg/Ca-rich) bulk chemistry than true flood/rift basalts or peridotite; included for completeness/data-gap tracking.
formations.existing_activity: None known for mineralization.
formations.source: General arc volcanism/geochemistry literature; no mineralization capacity study identified.

formations.name: Juan de Fuca Plate (offshore Pacific Northwest)
formations.capacity_basis: Goldberg et al. (2008, 2010) estimate total volume potential of the eastern + western Juan de Fuca Ridge flanks at 134-668 Gt-C (gigatonnes of carbon, for 20-100 m net reservoir thickness); converting Gt-C to GtCO2 (x3.67) gives roughly 490-2450 Gt CO2. A more conservative, commonly cited figure is 'at least 229 billion tonnes of carbon' (~840 Gt CO2) for the plate as a whole.
formations.depth_porosity_notes: Sub-seafloor pillow basalts with interconnected high-permeability zones and porosity up to ~20%; overlain by thick, low-permeability sediment providing a natural seal; multiple trapping mechanisms (hydrodynamic, CO2-density inversion at depth, and mineralization) reinforce containment.
formations.suitability_notes: One of the best-characterized offshore basalt targets globally; the Cascadia CarbonSAFE pre-feasibility study evaluated a 50 Mt CO2 / 20-year injection program on the eastern ridge flank ~200 miles offshore Washington/BC. Major caveat: offshore basalt storage costs are estimated in the ~$200-400/t CO2 range (POV estimate), far above onshore saline-aquifer storage costs, due to deepwater drilling and monitoring costs.
formations.existing_activity: Cascadia CarbonSAFE pre-feasibility study (Columbia Climate School / industry partners); no injection yet.
formations.source: Goldberg, D.S. et al. 2008 PNAS 'Carbon dioxide sequestration in deep-sea basalt'; Goldberg, D.S. et al. 2010 'Sub-seafloor Carbon Dioxide Storage Potential on the Juan de Fuca Plate' https://www.researchgate.net/publication/270955080_Sub-seafloor_Carbon_Dioxide_Storage_Potential_on_the_Juan_de_Fuca_Plate_Western_North_America; Columbia Climate School Cascadia Basin pre-feasibility project https://people.climate.columbia.edu/projects/view/1019

formations.name: Cascadia Basin / Offshore Washington-British Columbia sedimented basalt
formations.capacity_basis: Subset of the broader Juan de Fuca plate assessment (see above); the specific CarbonSAFE pre-feasibility target was a 50 Mt CO2 (0.05 Gt) 20-year injection program, far below the regional theoretical ceiling.
formations.depth_porosity_notes: Sediment-covered basalt basement, ~2600 m water depth, targeted interflow basalt reservoirs beneath several hundred meters of sediment (which also serves as a seal).
formations.suitability_notes: Practically-scoped, near-term project distinct from the plate-wide theoretical estimate; illustrates the gap between theoretical (Gt-scale) and project-scale (Mt-scale) numbers.
formations.existing_activity: Cascadia CarbonSAFE integrated pre-feasibility study completed; no injection to date.
formations.source: Columbia Climate School project page https://people.climate.columbia.edu/projects/view/1019

formations.name: Reykjanes Ridge / Offshore Iceland Mid-Atlantic Ridge basalt
formations.capacity_basis: Included within the broader Snæbjörnsdóttir & Gislason (2016) 60-7000 Gt Iceland+ridges estimate; a dedicated sub-seabed pilot on the Reykjanes Ridge itself has begun (2024 ScienceDirect paper on initial pilot results).
formations.depth_porosity_notes: Actively spreading mid-ocean ridge basalt, young and porous, directly offshore SW Iceland; shallower water depth than typical mid-ocean-ridge settings due to the Iceland hotspot's ridge elevation.
formations.suitability_notes: First sub-seabed basalt CO2 storage pilot on an active mid-ocean ridge segment; extends the onshore Carbfix approach offshore.
formations.existing_activity: Pilot project for sub-seabed basalt storage of CO2 on the Reykjanes Ridge (initial results reported 2024).
formations.source: ScienceDirect 2024 'Initial results of a pilot project for sub-seabed basalt storage of carbon dioxide on the Reykjanes Ridge' https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772656824000770

formations.name: Mid-Atlantic Ridge and global mid-ocean ridge system (generic offshore basalt)
formations.capacity_basis: SINTEF/NORDICCS synthesis (Snæbjörnsdóttir & Gislason 2016 and related work) states mid-ocean ridges worldwide could store 'up to 100,000 Gt CO2' in principle - an extreme upper-bound, whole-ridge-system theoretical figure, not a site-specific or economically meaningful estimate.
formations.depth_porosity_notes: Global mid-ocean ridge system basalt is universally young, porous, and reactive near spreading centers, but almost entirely inaccessible at present due to water depth (typically 2000-4000+ m) and distance from shore/CO2 sources.
formations.suitability_notes: Included as the theoretical global offshore-basalt ceiling referenced in the literature; essentially zero near-term practical relevance given extreme cost/distance (POV ~$200-400/t offshore is itself a best-case coastal figure, e.g. Juan de Fuca; true open-ocean ridge sites would be considerably more expensive).
formations.existing_activity: None (conceptual estimate only, apart from the localized Reykjanes Ridge pilot listed separately).
formations.source: Snæbjörnsdóttir, S.Ó. & Gislason, S.R. 2016 NORDICCS report https://www.sintef.no/globalassets/sintef-energi/nordiccs/d-6.1.1407-8-co2-storage-potential-of-basaltic-rocks-in-iceland-and-the-oceanic-ridges_web.pdf

formations.name: Vøring and Møre Basalt Margins (Offshore Norway)
formations.capacity_basis: No dedicated Gt capacity figure published yet; reservoir characterization (Rosenqvist et al. 2023 on Faroe Islands basalt as an onshore analogue for the Vøring/Møre offshore sequences, and direct Vøring Margin borehole [642E] data) is the current state of the science, precursor to any capacity estimate.
formations.depth_porosity_notes: Paleogene lava flows (0.6-18.5 m thick individual flows at borehole 642E) with porosity 0.5-36.2% concentrated in brecciated/vesicular flow-top crusts; buried beneath younger sediment on the Vøring and Møre margins of the Norwegian continental shelf.
formations.suitability_notes: Actively studied (CO2Basalt/Njord Centre, University of Oslo) as a potential complement to Norway's mature saline-aquifer CCS hubs (Northern Lights, Sleipner); notable because it targets the NAIP's offshore, buried lava sequence rather than exposed onshore basalt.
formations.existing_activity: Research/characterization stage only.
formations.source: Rosenqvist et al. 2023 https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1750583623000087; Njord CO2Basalt project page https://www.mn.uio.no/njord/english/research/projects/co2-basalts/index.html

formations.name: Kerguelen Plateau (Southern Indian Ocean)
formations.capacity_basis: No CO2 storage capacity study identified; the Kerguelen Plateau is one of the largest Large Igneous Provinces on Earth by volume (oceanic plateau basalt), but it is remote, deep-water, and has no nearby CO2 point sources.
formations.depth_porosity_notes: Submarine oceanic plateau basalt (Cretaceous), water depths mostly 1000-2000 m over the plateau itself; essentially unexplored for reservoir engineering purposes.
formations.suitability_notes: Included as a data-gap entry illustrating the scale mismatch between total global mafic rock volume and any realistically deployable subset; no practical near-term relevance.
formations.existing_activity: None.
formations.source: General Kerguelen Plateau geology literature (Frey et al., Coffin & Eldholm LIP reviews); no CO2 storage study identified.

formations.name: Harrat Al-Shaam / Arabian Harrat Basalt Fields (Saudi Arabia, Jordan)
formations.capacity_basis: Jizan region (SW Saudi Arabia) Oligocene basaltic rocks estimated at ~4.2 Gt CO2 total mineralization capacity, framed as sufficient for >100 years of regional industrial emissions; broader Harrat Al-Shaam basalt field (spanning Jordan/Saudi Arabia/Syria) and Harrat Rahat/Harrat Uwayrid are separately being assessed (2023-2025 studies) but not yet combined into one figure - low/high bounds here are an illustrative range pending a basin-wide synthesis.
formations.depth_porosity_notes: Cenozoic alkali/scoriaceous basalt fields (harrats) with vesicular, permeable flow tops interbedded with denser flow interiors; KAUST-led wettability/nanofluid-treatment studies characterize CO2/brine/basalt interfacial properties relevant to trapping capacity.
formations.suitability_notes: Part of the Saudi Green Initiative's CCS ambitions; KAUST explicitly frames its program as adapting the Icelandic Carbfix approach to Arabian basalt.
formations.existing_activity: Laboratory characterization (KAUST, various 2022-2025 papers on Jizan, Harrat Uwayrid, Harrat Rahat basalts); no field pilot yet identified.
formations.source: ScienceDirect 2022 'The subsurface carbonation potential of basaltic rocks from the Jizan region' https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1750583622001906; KAUST 'Storing Saudi Arabia's CO2' https://fii.kaust.edu.sa/en/energy-industrial-leadership/storing-saudi-arabia-c02/; ScienceDirect 2025 Harrat Al-Shaam Jordan study https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S2666261225000239; SPE 2025 Harrat Rahat scoriaceous basalt paper
